column7: BEATUS VIR QUI NON ABIIT IN CONSILIO IMPIORUM. Lapide: The psalm opens with a beatitude — the only proper beginning for a book of sacred songs. Literally this refers to David himself, who stood apart from the counsels of Abimelech, Achitophel, and the impious. Allegorically it refers above all to Christ, the perfectly Blessed Man, who never consented to sin even by the shadow of temptation. Tropologically it belongs to every just Christian. Note the triple descent: council (consilium) → way (via) → seat (cathedra). One first takes the wicked man's counsel, then walks in his way, then finally sits enthroned in pestilence as a teacher of evil. Lapide: "Non abiit... non stetit... non sedit" — three negatives marking a triple refusal. The godly man refuses even the first step. # GEM

QUARE FREMUERUNT GENTES. Lapide: The second psalm follows immediately as the companion to the first: the first shows the way of the just, the second shows the opposition of the nations to the Anointed (Christus) of the Lord. Peter and John quote this very verse in Acts 4:25 as fulfilled at the Passion of Christ. Lapide: the "tumult" (fremitus) of the nations = the noise of the Sanhedrin, Herod, and Pilate conspiring. "Et populi meditati sunt inania" — they meditated vanity, for their plot against Christ was futile; the Cross they intended as destruction became the instrument of universal salvation.

DOMINE DOMINUS NOSTER. Lapide: Ps. 8 is the great psalm of man's dignity and humility. "Domine Dominus noster" — Lord, our Lord: the double title marks both transcendence and intimacy. Quam admirabile est nomen tuum — the divine Name, which in Hebrew is YHWH, is wonderful (admirabile) because it expresses the fullness of being; the Name's glory fills the entire earth, which is the visible sacrament of the invisible majesty.

DEUS DEUS MEUS RESPICE IN ME. # GEM Lapide: This is the great Passion Psalm, cited from the Cross by Christ Himself (Mt. 27:46; Mk. 15:34). The literal sense in Lapide's approach has a dual subject: David in his tribulations prefigures Christ in His Passion, but the PROPER and principal subject is Christ Himself. The unanimous voice of the Fathers confirms this. The doubling "Deus Deus meus" expresses the intensity of Christ's desolation and simultaneously His unshaken faith: He does not say "my God was" but "my God IS" — the dereliction is real but not ultimate. Lapide: the cry from the Cross was Christ's human soul experiencing the withdrawal of the beatific overflow upon His lower powers, so that He might truly suffer for our sins — not a loss of the beatific vision itself, but of its consoling effects.

MISERERE MEI DEUS. # GEM Lapide: The title "Miserere" comes from the opening word — the greatest of the penitential psalms, the "queen of psalms" as Lapide calls it following Augustine. Historically: David composed this after the prophet Nathan rebuked him for the adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah (2 Sam. 12). Literally David's prayer; tropologically the prayer of every sinner. Allegorically it prefigures Christ assuming the voice of sinful humanity in the Incarnation. Lapide: "Secundum magnam misericordiam tuam" — the measure of the mercy asked corresponds to the measure of the divine mercy itself, not to any human standard. We do not ask for a small pardon proportionate to a small sin; we ask for mercy as great as God's own heart.

DIXIT DOMINUS DOMINO MEO: SEDE A DEXTRIS MEIS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 109 is the most frequently quoted Old Testament verse in the New Testament (Mt. 22:44; Mk. 12:36; Lk. 20:42-43; Acts 2:34-35; Heb. 1:13, 5:6, 7:17,21, 10:13; 1 Cor. 15:25; Eph. 1:20; Col. 3:1; 1 Pet. 3:22 and more). "The Lord said to my Lord" — David, inspired by the Holy Spirit, calls the Messiah "my Lord" (Domino meo). Christ uses this against the Pharisees (Mt. 22:41-46): if the Messiah is merely David's physical descendant, why does David call him "Lord"? They cannot answer. Lapide: the first "Dominus" = the Father; the second "Domino meo" = the Son, who is David's Lord because He is God. This verse establishes the Divinity of the Messiah from the mouth of David himself. "Sede a dextris meis" — the right hand = equality of nature, glory, and power with the Father. The Ascension and Session at the right hand fulfilled (Acts 2:33).

DE PROFUNDIS CLAMAVI AD TE DOMINE. # GEM Lapide: The De Profundis — one of the seven penitential psalms, and the psalm par excellence for the souls in Purgatory. Literally the psalm refers to David crying from the depths of affliction, captivity, or sin. Tropologically it is the prayer of every sinner. But Lapide gives extended treatment to the anagogical sense: the souls in Purgatory cry from the "depths" (de profundis) of their place of purification. They cannot directly pray for themselves (being beyond merit), but their cry reaches God through the suffrages of the Church — Masses, prayers, alms, indulgences applied to them. The Catholic practice of reciting this psalm for the faithful departed is directly rooted in Lapide's tradition. # GEM

DOMINE QUID MULTIPLICATI SUNT. Lapide: Psalm 3: A psalm of David when he fled from Absalom his son (title). Literally: David surrounded by enemies on the day of Absalom's rebellion. Allegorically: Christ surrounded by enemies at the Passion. Tropologically: every soul under spiritual attack. "Quam multi insurgunt adversum me" — the multiplication of enemies is a sign not of God's absence but often of one's growth in holiness and public influence, which attracts opposition.

CUM INVOCAREM EXAUDIVIT ME DEUS. Lapide: Psalm 4 is an evening psalm (used at Compline). "When I called upon Him, the God of my justice heard me." Lapide: "Deus iustitiae meae" — God of my justice: the God who vindicates the just. Lapide notes that prayer is always heard by God (though the answer may be delayed or different from what we expect). The primary answer to prayer is not always the removal of suffering but the gift of grace to bear it.

VERBA MEA AURIBUS PERCIPE DOMINE. Lapide: Psalm 5 is a morning psalm. "Give ear to my words, O Lord, understand my cry." Lapide: the soul begins the day with prayer, offering its words and groans to God. The distinction: verba = articulate prayer, meditatio = interior groaning beyond words. Both are heard by God, who searches the heart.

DOMINE NE IN FURORE TUO ARGUAS ME. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 6 — the first of the seven penitential psalms. "O Lord, rebuke me not in Thy fury, nor chastise me in Thy anger." Lapide: David (and every penitent) does not pray to escape all correction — divine chastisement is medicinal and loving (Heb. 12:6). He prays to be corrected in mercy, not in wrath. Lapide distinguishes: the wrath of justice (which the damned endure) from the corrective mercy (which the sinner on earth experiences). We flee not from God but to God.

CONSERVA ME DOMINE QUONIAM SPERAVI IN TE. Lapide: Psalm 15 (Michtam of David) — Peter cites this psalm in Acts 2:25-28 as fulfilled in Christ's Resurrection. Lapide: the psalm is literally a prayer of David in danger, but principally and properly a prophecy of Christ. "Preserve me, O Lord, for I have put my trust in Thee." The trust of Christ's human soul in the Father throughout His Passion.

DILIGAM TE DOMINE FORTITUDO MEA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 17 — the great thanksgiving psalm of David (parallel 2 Sam. 22). "I will love Thee, O Lord, my strength." Lapide notes the future tense "diligam" — not merely "I love Thee" but "I WILL love Thee," expressing a firm resolution and commitment of the will, not merely a passing sentiment. Love of God is an act of the will (dilectio), not merely of the emotions. Lapide: the six divine titles that follow ("my strength, my rock, my deliverer, my God, my refuge, my shield, the horn of my salvation, my support") are a theology of divine Providence in miniature.

CAELI ENARRANT GLORIAM DEI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 18: "The heavens show forth the glory of God." One of the great texts of natural theology. Lapide: the created order — visible and ordered, vast and beautiful — is a book of divine revelation. The invisible attributes of God (His power, wisdom, beauty, providence) are made visible through the things He has made (Rom. 1:20). Lapide gives an extended meditation: the heavens speak without words (v.4: "there are no speeches nor languages where their voices are not heard") — a universal, silent proclamation accessible to every rational creature. The theological proof of God's existence from the beauty of creation is not an addition to faith but a preamble to it.

MEMOR SIT DOMINUS OMNIS SACRIFICII TUI. Lapide: Psalm 19 — a prayer for the king before battle. "May the Lord be mindful of all thy sacrifices." Lapide: literally a prayer for David. Allegorically: the Church's prayer for Christ entering His Passion — may the Father accept the sacrifice of the Cross. Tropologically: the Church's prayer for her members entering the battles of spiritual warfare.

DOMINE IN VIRTUTE TUA LAETABITUR REX. Lapide: Psalm 20 — thanksgiving after victory. "In Thy strength, O Lord, the king shall joy." The king = Christ. Lapide: "et super salutare tuum exsultabit vehementer" — the human soul of Christ rejoices in the Father's salvation, the Resurrection and Ascension. Lapide: every Christian king and ruler should recognize that all strength and victory comes from God, not from their own power.

DOMINUS REGIT ME ET NIHIL MIHI DEERIT. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 22 (Vulgate) — the Good Shepherd Psalm. "The Lord rules me and nothing shall be wanting to me." This verse is the complete summary of Christian confidence in Providence. Lapide: "regit" — the Lord does not merely assist or advise, He RULES (regit): He is the active shepherd, director, and governor of the soul. "Nihil mihi deerit" — nothing shall be lacking to me. Lapide: this is not a promise of material prosperity but of the sufficiency of grace. Everything the soul truly needs (not necessarily desires) shall be provided by the divine Shepherd.

DOMINI EST TERRA ET PLENITUDO EIUS. Lapide: Psalm 23 — the processional psalm for the Ark. "The earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof." Lapide: all creation belongs to God; man is a steward, not an owner. This verse is the root of the social doctrine of property: while private ownership is natural and good, it is always relativized by the prior claim of the Creator.

AD TE DOMINE LEVAVI ANIMAM MEAM. Lapide: Psalm 24 (acrostic in Hebrew) — "To Thee, O Lord, I have lifted up my soul." The lifting of the soul = the posture of prayer and hope. Lapide: this is the great prayer of hope — not merely intellectual assent but the whole soul directed upward to God.

BEATI QUORUM REMISSAE SUNT INIQUITATES. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 31 — the second penitential psalm. Paul quotes vv.1-2 in Rom. 4:7-8 to establish the doctrine of justification by faith: "Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven and whose sins are covered." Lapide: "remissae sunt" (are FORGIVEN) and "tecta sunt" (are COVERED) — two metaphors for forgiveness: (1) the debt is remitted/cancelled; (2) the sin is covered by God's mercy, so that He no longer sees it against us. The third (v.2): "beatus vir cui non imputavit Dominus peccatum" — blessed the man to whom the Lord has not imputed sin. Lapide against the Protestant reading: this non-imputation is not merely forensic; it corresponds to a real interior change — the infusion of sanctifying grace by which sin is truly (not merely legally) removed.

EXSULTATE IUSTI IN DOMINO. Lapide: Psalm 32 — a hymn of praise. "Rejoice in the Lord, O ye just." Lapide: the call to rejoice is not an invitation but a command (imperative). Joy in God is an obligation of justice for those who have received His gifts. Lapide notes: the praise of God is both a duty and a privilege — a duty because God deserves it infinitely; a privilege because the creature is honored to give it.

BENEDICAM DOMINUM IN OMNI TEMPORE. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 33 — "I will bless the Lord at all times: His praise shall be always in my mouth." Lapide: "in omni tempore" — at all times, not only in prosperity and consolation but also in adversity and desolation. The continual praise of God is both the goal of the liturgy and the mark of the perfect soul. Lapide: this is the spirit of the Divine Office, which distributes prayer through all the hours of the day and night, so that the Church, spread throughout the world, is always blessing God somewhere.

IUDICA DOMINE NOCENTES ME. Lapide: Psalm 34 — a prayer against persecutors, fulfilled in Christ's Passion. "Judge them, O Lord, that wrong me." Lapide: this imprecatory psalm (calling down judgment on enemies) must be understood in the typological framework: it is Christ speaking, and the judgment invoked is not personal revenge but divine justice exercised through history (the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD as a consequence of the Passion).

DIXIT INIUSTUS UT DELINQUAT IN SEMETIPSO. Lapide: Psalm 35 — the wickedness of the impious. "The unjust hath said within himself that he would sin." Lapide: sin begins in the heart, in the interior dialogue of the will with itself. The wicked man "says" (dixit) — he makes an interior resolution to sin, the complete opposite of the psalmist who "blesses God at all times." Lapide: "non est timor Dei ante oculos eius" (there is no fear of God before his eyes, v.2) — Paul quotes this in Romans 3:18 as the root of all sin.

DELECTARE IN DOMINO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 36 — "Delight in the Lord, and He will give thee the requests of thy heart." Lapide: "delectare in Domino" — to delight in God is the supreme act of the theological virtue of love. When the will's deepest delight is God Himself, the requests of the heart (which are now ordered to God) are identical with what God wills, and so they are always granted. Augustine's great principle: "Love and do what you will" — when love is properly ordered to God, the will spontaneously wills what God wills.

NON EST SANITAS IN CARNE MEA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 37 — penitential psalm. "There is no health in my flesh because of Thy wrath: there is no peace for my bones because of my sins." Lapide: the psalmist experiences the bodily effects of sin — not as divine punishment of a particular sin but as the general condition of fallen humanity, whose body is "subject to vanity" (Rom. 8:20). The sickness of the body points to the deeper sickness of the soul. Lapide: "a facie irae tuae" — the face of divine wrath is the terrifying prospect that the penitent faces squarely, without self-deception.

CALUIT COR MEUM INTRA ME. Lapide: Psalm 38 — "My heart grew hot within me: and in my meditation a fire shall flame out." Lapide: the prophet's burning interior compels him to speak. Silence before the wicked (vv.1-3) becomes impossible when God's fire burns within. Lapide applies this to the prophet, the preacher, the spiritual director: sometimes prudent silence before those who cannot hear; but the interior fire of God's word eventually compels speech.

HOLOCAUSTUM ET PRO PECCATO NON POSTULASTI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 39 — cited in Heb. 10:5-7 as spoken by Christ at the Incarnation. "Sacrifice and oblation Thou didst not desire... holocausts and sin-offerings Thou didst not require. Then said I: Behold I come." Lapide: the Incarnation as the replacement of the Old Law's sacrifices. The body prepared for Christ (Heb. 10:5: "a body Thou hast fitted to me" — LXX version) is the instrument of the one perfect sacrifice. Lapide: "In capite libri scriptum est de me" — "In the head of the book it is written of me": Christ is the subject of the entire Old Testament, from Genesis onward; the whole of Scripture points to Him.

BEATUS QUI INTELLEGIT SUPER EGENUM ET PAUPEREM. Lapide: Psalm 40 — "Blessed is he that understandeth concerning the needy and the poor: the Lord will deliver him." Lapide: "intellegit" — not merely feels sympathy but UNDERSTANDS: sees the needy person in their dignity as God's image, discerns their true need (not always what they ask for), and acts accordingly. The social duty of the strong toward the weak is here given its theological foundation: care for the poor is the test of authentic faith.

QUEMADMODUM DESIDERAT CERVUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 41 — "As the hart panteth after the fountains of water: so my soul panteth after Thee, O God." One of the most beautiful expressions of mystical longing in Scripture. Lapide follows Augustine's famous commentary: the hart (cervus) was believed to draw serpents from their holes by its breath and devour them; having filled itself with venom, it thirsts desperately for water to quench the burning. So the soul, filled with the poison of sin and worldly desires, burns with thirst for God alone. Lapide: this verse is used liturgically at baptism (the newly baptized panting for the living waters they have just received), and in mystic theology as the archetype of contemplative longing.

ERUCTAVIT COR MEUM VERBUM BONUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 44 — the royal wedding psalm, the most explicitly Messianic love-poem in the Psalter. "My heart hath uttered a good word: I speak my works to the King." Lapide: the "good word" = the eternal Word (Verbum), the Son of God. The psalmist's heart "eructates" (belches forth) the Word — a vivid image from the belly, suggesting both fullness and spontaneous overflow. The King of the psalm is the divine Messiah; the Queen (v.10) is the Church, the Bride of Christ. Lapide gives an extended typological commentary drawing on the Song of Songs.

DEUS NOSTER REFUGIUM ET VIRTUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 45 — the great "Fortress" psalm. "Our God is our refuge and strength, a helper in tribulations." Luther made this psalm the basis of "Ein feste Burg ist unser Gott," which Lapide (writing in the same era) implicitly counters by showing the Catholic interpretation: the fortress is not merely a metaphor of individual trust but the Church herself — "civitas Dei" (city of God), the visible community of the faithful gathered around the sacraments.

OMNES GENTES PLAUDITE MANIBUS. Lapide: Psalm 46 — "O clap your hands, all ye nations: shout unto God with the voice of joy." The universal call to praise. Lapide: "omnes gentes" — all nations, not merely Israel. The universalism of Christian worship: every people and culture called to render joyful homage to God. Lapide notes this is fulfilled in the Church's mission (Acts 2 at Pentecost: all nations hearing in their own language).

MONS SION LATERA AQUILONIS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 47 — "Mount Sion, on the sides of the north, the city of the great King." The theological geography of Jerusalem. Lapide: Jerusalem on the north side of Sion is a figure of the Church, the city of the great King (Mt. 5:35 — Christ calls Jerusalem "the city of the great King"). Lapide: the Church is built on the foundation of Mount Sion — the apostolic tradition and the Petrine primacy.

HOMO CUM IN HONORE ESSET NON INTELLEXIT. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 48 — "Man when he was in honour did not understand: he hath been compared to senseless beasts and made like to them." The tragedy of original sin: man, created in honor and dignity (cf. Ps. 8:6), did not understand his vocation and sank to the level of the beast through sin. Lapide: this verse is cited in the Office of the Dead as a meditation on mortality and judgment. The refrain (v.13 and v.21) — repeated like a toll of the bell — calls every soul to remember its dignity and its duty.

DEUS DEORUM DOMINUS LOCUTUS EST. Lapide: Psalm 49 — the great judgment scene. "The God of gods, the Lord hath spoken, and called the earth from the rising of the sun to the going down thereof." Lapide: "Deus deorum" — God of gods: the supreme God above all whom men worship (true or false). The universal summons of all creation to the divine tribunal. Lapide: this is a figure of the Last Judgment, in which God calls the earth to account.

QUID GLORIARIS IN MALITIA. Lapide: Psalm 51 — addressed (in the title) to the treacherous Doeg the Edomite who betrayed the priests of Nob to Saul (1 Sam. 22). Lapide: literally against Doeg; tropologically against all who boast of their wickedness and use the tongue as a weapon. "Lingua tua sicut novacula acuta" (v.4) — the tongue sharp as a razor: the destructive power of lying, detraction, and slander. Lapide gives a full excursus on the sins of the tongue.

DEUS IN NOMINE TUO SALVUM ME FAC. Lapide: Psalm 53 — a brief prayer for help. "Save me, O God, by Thy Name." Lapide: the Name of God (Nomen Dei) = the divine nature and attributes invoked as the ground of salvation. Not merely a formula but the actual divine reality to which prayer is addressed. Lapide connects this to Jn. 14:13: "Whatever you shall ask the Father in My name..."

IACTA IN DOMINUM CURAM TUAM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 54 — "Cast thy care upon the Lord, and He shall sustain thee." Peter quotes this in 1 Pet. 5:7. Lapide: "iacta" — cast, throw with force, as one throws a heavy burden. The image is of a traveler throwing a heavy pack onto a beast of burden. Spiritual abandonment to Providence: the soul does not carry its anxieties alone but throws them onto God, who willingly takes them. "Ipse te enutriet" — He will nourish/sustain thee. The divine care is nutritive, feeding the soul through every trial.

PARATUM COR MEUM DEUS. Lapide: Psalm 56 — "My heart is ready, O God, my heart is ready." Lapide: the twice-repeated "paratum" (ready) marks the soul's firm resolution and complete orientation toward God. Ready for what? For praise (vv.8-9), for witness, for whatever God wills. Lapide: readiness of heart = the fundamental disposition of the will fully surrendered to God, the posture of "fiat" — Thy will be done.

DEUS DEUS MEUS AD TE DE LUCE VIGILO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 62 — the morning psalm of longing. "O God, my God, to Thee do I watch at break of day." The morning prayer as encounter. Lapide: "ad te de luce vigilo" — I watch for Thee from the first light. The contemplative soul anticipates the dawn, rises before it, watches for God as for the rising sun. "Sitivit in te anima mea, quam multipliciter tibi caro mea" (v.2 — Vulgate) — my soul has thirsted for Thee; my flesh also, in how many ways, for Thee. Lapide: the entire person — soul and body — directed toward God in longing.

EXSURGAT DEUS ET DISSIPENTUR INIMICI EIUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 67 — the great processional psalm. "Let God arise, and let His enemies be scattered." Paul quotes a verse from this psalm (v.18) in Eph. 4:8. Lapide: primarily a psalm for the procession of the Ark; allegorically the Resurrection ("Exsurgat Deus" = God arises from the dead) and the Ascension. The scattering of enemies = the dispersal of sin, death, and the devil by the power of the Resurrection.

SALVUM ME FAC DEUS QUONIAM INTRAVERUNT AQUAE USQUE AD ANIMAM MEAM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 68 — the great Passion psalm (second after Ps. 21). "Save me, O God: for the waters are come in even unto my soul." Lapide: the waters of tribulation and death reaching the soul = Christ in the Passion, overwhelmed by the torrent of human sin He bore. The psalm is cited repeatedly in the New Testament as fulfilled in the Passion (vv.5,9,21-22 in the Gospels; v.26 in Acts 1:20; vv.22-24 in Rom. 11:9-10).

QUONIAM TU ES PATIENTIA MEA DOMINE. Lapide: Psalm 70 — a psalm of old age and trust. "For Thou art my patience, O Lord: my hope from my youth." Lapide: God is not merely the object of patience but its source — He is the patient man's patience. The theological virtue of hope (spes mea) grounded in God alone. Lapide: this verse is especially beautiful for the elderly who have "hoped from youth" — whose entire life has been a school of hope in God's fidelity.

DEUS IUDICIUM TUUM REGI DA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 71 — the great Messianic royal psalm. "Give to the king Thy judgment, O God: and to the king's son Thy justice." Lapide: the King and the King's Son = the Messiah, the eternal Son of David. The psalm describes the universal reign of the just king in terms that exceed any historical monarch: he shall reign from sea to sea (v.8), all kings shall adore him (v.11), all nations shall serve him (v.11), his name shall endure before the sun (v.17). Lapide: no human king fulfills these conditions; only Christ.

QUID ENIM MIHI EST IN CAELO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 72 — the great theodicy psalm. "For what have I in heaven, and besides Thee what do I desire upon earth?" Lapide: the conclusion of the psalm after the psalmist's crisis of faith (vv.1-14: why do the wicked prosper?). Having been shown the end of the wicked (v.17) in God's sanctuary, the psalmist arrives at the highest mystical affirmation: God alone suffices. "What have I in heaven?" — no created good in heaven (not even the angels, not even bodily beatitude) satisfies the soul's deepest longing; only God Himself. Lapide: "Deus cordis mei et pars mea Deus in aeternum" (v.26) — God of my heart and my portion forever. The beatific vision as the soul's only and total good.

APERIAM IN PARABOLIS OS MEUM. Lapide: Psalm 77 (Asaph) — "I will open my mouth in parables: I will utter propositions from the beginning." Matthew quotes v.2 as fulfilled in Christ's teaching in parables (Mt. 13:35). Lapide: the prophetic tradition of teaching by parable runs from Moses through the psalms to Christ, in whom all parabolic speech finds its fullest expression and explanation.

QUAM DILECTA TABERNACULA TUA DOMINE. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 83 — the great pilgrim psalm. "How lovely are Thy tabernacles, O Lord of hosts!" Lapide: one of his most lyrical commentaries. The "tabernacles" = the Temple courts, and allegorically the Church with her liturgy and sacraments, and ultimately heaven itself. The longing of the soul (v.3: "my soul longeth and fainteth for the courts of the Lord") is the fundamental spiritual posture of the pilgrim Church: present in the world, longing for the heavenly Temple.

BENEDIXISTI DOMINE TERRAM TUAM. Lapide: Psalm 84 — "Thou hast blessed Thy land, O Lord: Thou hast turned away the captivity of Jacob." Lapide: literally the return from Babylonian exile; allegorically the Incarnation as God's supreme blessing upon the earth — "the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us" (Jn. 1:14). The land blessed = the human nature assumed by the Son of God.

TU DOMINE SUAVIS ET MITIS. Lapide: Psalm 85 — "For Thou, O Lord, art sweet and mild, and plenteous in mercy to all that call upon Thee." The three divine attributes: sweetness (suavis), mildness (mitis), and abundant mercy (multus in misericordia). Lapide: these are the qualities of God as experienced by the devout soul in prayer — not the terrible justice of the judge but the tender mercy of the Father. Christ called Himself "meek and humble of heart" (Mt. 11:29) — these attributes of the Father are incarnated in the Son.

DOMINE DEUS SALUTIS MEAE. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 87 — the darkest psalm, the psalm of desolation. "O Lord, the God of my salvation: I have cried in the day, and in the night before Thee." Lapide: this psalm, more than any other, is applied to Christ's cry from the Cross — the "dark night of the soul" at its most extreme. Verses 5-6: "I am as a man without help, free among the dead... whom Thou rememberest no more" — Christ experiencing the uttermost dereliction of spirit. Lapide: the "dark night" described by mystics (John of the Cross, etc.) is rooted in this psalm. The soul in desolation should take comfort that Christ Himself experienced and sanctified this state.

MISERICORDIAS DOMINI IN AETERNUM CANTABO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 88 — the great covenant psalm. "The mercies of the Lord I will sing forever." Lapide: the eternal song of divine mercy — the proper occupation of the soul in this life and in the life to come. The entire psalm is a meditation on God's covenant with David (vv.4-5, 20-38), which is fulfilled in Christ the eternal Son of David. Lapide: "In generationem et generationem" — the mercy of God spans every generation, never exhausted, always new.

DOMINE REFUGIUM FACTUS ES NOBIS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 89 — the great psalm of Moses, the only psalm attributed to Moses in the Psalter. "Lord, Thou hast been our refuge from generation to generation." Lapide: God as the permanent dwelling-place of His people: before the mountains were made, before the earth was formed, God was their home. Lapide: this verse is the foundation of the doctrine of divine aseity and eternity — God exists independently of all creation; He is the "refuge" (domus) of the soul because He is the uncreated ground of all creaturely being.

QUI HABITAT IN ADIUTORIO ALTISSIMI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 90 — "He that dwelleth in the aid of the most High, shall abide under the protection of the God of Jacob." Lapide: the great psalm of divine protection, cited by the devil in the temptation of Christ (Mt. 4:6 — "for He hath given His angels charge over thee"). Lapide: the devil misquoted and misapplied this psalm; Christ corrected him by quoting Deut. 6:16. The psalm promises protection to those who DWELL in God (qui habitat) — not to those who test Him. Lapide: habitation in God = the state of sanctifying grace; the angels protect those who are in that state.

BONUM EST CONFITERI DOMINO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 91 — the Sabbath psalm. "It is good to give praise to the Lord: and to sing to Thy name, O Most High." Lapide: "Bonum est" — it is GOOD (morally excellent, ontologically fitting) to praise God. The divine praise is not merely pleasant or beneficial for the soul (though it is both) but ontologically appropriate: it is the creature's proper response to the Creator. Lapide: "To confess" (confiteri) = to praise; the same word means both praise and acknowledgment of sin — both are forms of truth-telling before God.

DOMINE EXAUDI ORATIONEM MEAM. Lapide: Psalm 101 — the fifth penitential psalm. "Hear, O Lord, my prayer: and let my cry come to Thee." Lapide: a psalm of the Babylonian exile, expressing the affliction of the holy community. Allegorically: the prayer of Christ in His Passion; and the prayer of the souls in Purgatory. "Inclina ad me aurem tuam" — bend thine ear: the image of God bowing down from heaven to hear the faint voice of the suffering creature. Lapide: divine condescension (katabasis) in hearing prayer.

BENEDIC ANIMA MEA DOMINO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 102 — the great mercy psalm. "Bless the Lord, O my soul: and let all that is within me bless His holy name." Lapide: the soul is commanded by the psalmist (David speaking to himself) to bless God — all its faculties, all its interior parts (memory, intellect, will, imagination, affections). The wholeness of the blessing demanded: nothing withheld from God's praise. "Et noli oblivisci omnes retributiones eius" (v.2) — forget not all His benefits: ingratitude is the root of sin; the remembrance of God's benefits is the root of praise.

BENEDIC ANIMA MEA DOMINO DOMINE DEUS MEUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 103 — the great hymn of creation. "Bless the Lord, O my soul: O Lord my God, Thou art exceedingly great." Lapide: this psalm is the scriptural basis of all Christian natural theology and the theology of creation. Verses 1-4 describe the divine majesty; vv.5-9 the creation of the earth and seas; vv.10-18 the care for living creatures; vv.19-23 the ordering of time (moon and sun); vv.24-30 the wisdom of creation; vv.31-35 the praise of God. Lapide devotes extensive commentary to this psalm.

CONFITEBOR TIBI DOMINE IN TOTO CORDE MEO. Lapide: Psalm 110 — "I will praise Thee, O Lord, with my whole heart, in the council of the just, and in the congregation." Lapide: the whole heart (toto corde) = the total gift of all the soul's faculties in praise. The context: "in the council of the just and in the congregation" — the liturgical assembly. Private prayer is good; liturgical public prayer is more excellent because it is the prayer of the whole Body of Christ.

BEATUS VIR QUI TIMET DOMINUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 111 — the beatitude of the one who fears God. "Blessed is the man that feareth the Lord: he shall delight exceedingly in His commandments." The fear of the Lord as the root of all human flourishing. Lapide: this psalm describes the perfect Christian — generous to the poor (v.9), just in business, stable in heart (v.7), not fearing bad news (v.7), his horn exalted in glory (v.9). Lapide: the "horn exalted" = the resurrection of the body; the goods of the just man in this psalm are both temporal (prosperity, stability) and eternal.

LAUDATE PUERI DOMINUM. Lapide: Psalm 112 — the first of the Hallel psalms (sung at Passover). "Praise the Lord, ye children: praise ye the name of the Lord." Lapide: "pueri" = (1) literally young people; (2) allegorically those young in virtue and beginners in the spiritual life; (3) tropologically all the faithful who are "children of God" by adoption. The universal call to praise beginning with the humblest.

IN EXITU ISRAEL DE AEGYPTO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 113 — the Exodus psalm. "When Israel went out of Egypt, the house of Jacob from a barbarous people." Dante takes this psalm (the Italian begins "In exitu Israel de Aegypto") as the model of the fourfold sense of Scripture in the Convivio. Lapide follows the tradition: (1) literal = the historical Exodus; (2) allegorical = the redemption from sin by Christ (Egypt = sin; Pharaoh = the devil; crossing the Red Sea = baptism); (3) tropological = the soul's ongoing exodus from vice to virtue; (4) anagogical = the soul's passage at death from this world to heaven.

LAUDATE DOMINUM OMNES GENTES. Lapide: Psalm 116 — the shortest psalm and the most universal: "Praise the Lord, all ye nations: praise Him all ye people." Paul quotes v.1 in Rom. 15:11 as foretelling the Gentile mission. Lapide: the praise of all nations fulfills the Abrahamic covenant (Gen. 12:3). The brevity of the psalm is itself significant: the call to universal praise is absolute and unconditional, requiring no qualification or restriction.

BEATI IMMACULATI IN VIA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 118 — the great acrostic psalm (22 strophes of 8 verses each, following the Hebrew alphabet), the longest psalm and the great meditation on the divine Law. "Blessed are the undefiled in the way: who walk in the law of the Lord." Lapide devotes enormous attention to this psalm, covering it over many pages. The Law (Torah/lex) is not experienced as burden but as gift and delight. Lapide: the eight synonyms for the divine Law used throughout the psalm (lex, testimonium, mandatum, iudicium, praeceptum, via, eloquium, iustificatio) show the inexhaustible richness of the divine word.

AD DOMINUM CUM TRIBULARER CLAMAVI. Lapide: Psalm 119 (the first of the 15 Gradual Psalms, Psalms 119-133) — "In my trouble I cried to the Lord, and He heard me." Lapide: the Gradual Psalms (Psalmi Graduum, Cantica Graduum) were sung by pilgrims ascending the 15 steps of the Temple, or on the journey up to Jerusalem for the great feasts. Allegorically: the soul's ascent through the degrees of prayer and virtue toward the heavenly Jerusalem. Each psalm corresponds to a step in the spiritual life.

LEVAVI OCULOS MEOS IN MONTES. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 120 — "I have lifted up my eyes to the mountains, from whence help shall come to me." The mountains = Sion and Moriah (the Temple mount) to which the pilgrim looks for help; allegorically = the heavenly Jerusalem, the saints, the angels; primarily = God Himself (v.2: "My help is from the Lord, who made heaven and earth"). Lapide: the lifting of the eyes (levavi oculos) = the act of hope — turning the soul's gaze upward from earth to heaven. This psalm has consoled generations of travelers and pilgrims throughout the Church's history.

LAETATUS SUM IN HIS QUAE DICTA SUNT MIHI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 121 — "I rejoiced at the things that were said to me: we shall go into the house of the Lord." The joy of the pilgrim who hears "Let us go to Jerusalem." Lapide: literally the joy of the Israelite summoned to the pilgrimage feasts. Allegorically: the soul's joy at the summons to enter the Church through faith and the sacraments. Anagogically: the supreme joy of the dying soul told by the Church's final rites "we shall go into the house of the Lord" — the heavenly Jerusalem. Lapide: Augustine wrote his most beautiful meditations on this verse: the joy of going to the eternal city.

AD TE LEVAVI OCULOS MEOS. Lapide: Psalm 122 — "To Thee have I lifted up my eyes, who dwellest in heaven." The posture of prayer: the soul's gaze fixed on the heavenly God. Lapide: "sicut oculi servorum in manibus dominorum suorum" (v.2) — as the eyes of servants are on the hands of their masters. The image of attentive service: watching the master's hands for the slightest gesture of command or invitation. Lapide: this is the model of contemplative attentiveness to God's will.

ADIUTORIUM NOSTRUM IN NOMINE DOMINI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 123 — "Our help is in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth." The concluding doxology that has become the traditional opening of Catholic liturgical functions. Lapide: "Adjutorium nostrum" — our help (not merely my help but OUR: the communal dimension). The Name of the Lord = the divine nature and perfections. "Qui fecit caelum et terram" = the Creator God, whose power over His creation is the ground of our confidence that He can help us.

QUI SEMINANT IN LACRIMIS IN EXSULTATIONE METENT. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 125 — "They that sow in tears shall reap in joy." One of the most beloved verses of the Psalter. Lapide: the seed (semen) = good works, prayers, penances, sacrifices sown in the "tears" of tribulation, spiritual dryness, suffering. The harvest = the joy of eternal life, the beatific vision. Lapide: no tear shed for God is wasted — each one is a seed in the ground of divine providence that will bring forth fruit in eternal joy. This verse is the theology of Christian suffering in a single image.

NISI DOMINUS AEDIFICAVERIT DOMUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 126 — "Unless the Lord build the house, they labour in vain that build it." The absolute dependence of all human endeavor on divine assistance. Lapide: the "house" = (1) the Temple of Jerusalem; (2) the Church; (3) the individual soul; (4) the family and social institutions. Without God at the foundation, all human construction is futile. "Nisi Dominus custodierit civitatem, frustra vigilat qui custodit eam" — Unless God guards the city, the watchman watches in vain. Lapide: the political theology of dependence on divine Providence.

BEATI OMNES QUI TIMENT DOMINUM. Lapide: Psalm 127 — "Blessed are all they that fear the Lord, that walk in His ways." The beatitude of the God-fearing household. Lapide: vv.1-2 describe the blessed husband (blessed by his own labor); vv.3-4 the blessed wife (fruitful as a vine); v.3 the blessed children (as olive plants around the table). Lapide: this psalm is the theology of the Christian family in miniature — ordered to God (fear of the Lord), fruitful (children as olive plants), prosperous (eating the fruit of one's hands), and hopeful (seeing Jerusalem's good all the days of one's life, v.5).

DOMINE NON EST EXALTATUM COR MEUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 130 — "Lord, my heart is not exalted: nor are my eyes lofty. Neither have I walked in great things, nor in wonders above me." The psalm of perfect humility. Lapide: the three renunciations — of proud thought (heart not exalted), proud look (eyes not lofty), proud ambition (great things, wonders beyond capacity). Lapide: this brief psalm (3 verses) is the condensed theology of the virtue of humility. "As a child that is weaned is towards his mother, so is my soul within me" (v.2) — the image of the weaned child who is content with the mother's presence without demanding the breast: the soul at peace with God without seeking consolations, the beginning of pure love.

SURGE DOMINE IN REQUIEM TUAM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 131 — "Arise, O Lord, into Thy resting place: Thou and the Ark, which Thou hast sanctified." The prayer for the Ark's installation in the Temple. Lapide: "Surge Domine" = the Resurrection of Christ ("Arise, Lord" from the tomb). The "Ark which Thou hast sanctified" = the Blessed Virgin Mary, who as the Ark contained the true presence of God incarnate. Lapide: Mary as the New Ark is one of the most ancient Mariological types, rooted in this psalm. The "resting place" (requiem) = the womb of Mary, the Church, and ultimately heaven.

ECCE QUAM BONUM ET QUAM IUCUNDUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 132 — "Behold how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity." Lapide: the great psalm of fraternal charity, the scriptural basis of monastic and religious community life. "Bonum et iucundum" — both good (morally excellent) and pleasant (experientially delightful): fraternal unity is not dour duty but genuine joy. Lapide: the comparison to the precious ointment poured on Aaron's head and the dew of Hermon — both images of superabundance and overflowing: true charity cannot be contained but flows from person to person, from community to world.

BENEDICITE DOMINUM OMNES SERVI DOMINI. Lapide: Psalm 133 — the last of the Gradual Psalms; the evening prayer at the Temple. "Praise ye the Lord, all ye servants of the Lord: who stand in the house of the Lord, in the courts of the house of our God." Lapide: the servants (servi) = the Levites and priests who minister through the night in the Temple; allegorically = the monks and nuns who keep the night Office; tropologically = all the faithful who pray before sleep. "Benedicite Dominum" — bless the Lord as the last act of the day, entrusting the night to His care.

SUPER FLUMINA BABYLONIS ILLIC SEDIMUS ET FLEVIMUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 136 — the great exile psalm. "Upon the rivers of Babylon, there we sat and wept, when we remembered Sion." Lapide: the pathos of exile is one of Scripture's most powerful images. Literally: the Jewish exiles in Babylon (587 BC). Allegorically: the soul in the exile of this life, weeping for the heavenly Jerusalem. Tropologically: the soul in the exile of sin, weeping for lost grace and union with God. Lapide: "Illic sedimus et flevimus" — "there we SAT and WEPT": the helplessness of exile expressed in the posture of sitting (one who has given up moving) and weeping (one who has given up hope of return on their own). The only resource: God's fidelity to His promise of return.

DOMINE PROBASTI ME ET COGNOVISTI ME. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 138 — the great psalm of divine omniscience. "Lord, Thou hast proved me and known me: Thou hast known my sitting down and my rising up." Lapide: this is the most extended meditation on divine omniscience in Scripture. God knows every movement of the soul — sitting, rising, going out, coming in (vv.1-3). He knows the words before they are formed (v.4). He is before and behind (v.5). Lapide: this "knowledge" is not merely intellectual but creative and sustaining: God knows me because He made me and continuously holds me in being. "Thou hast known from afar" (v.2) = from eternity, before I existed.

ERIPE ME DOMINE AB HOMINE MALO. Lapide: Psalm 139 — "Deliver me, O Lord, from the evil man: rescue me from the unjust man." Lapide: literally a prayer of David against Saul or Achitophel; allegorically the prayer of Christ against the devil and the Sanhedrin; tropologically the prayer of every Christian against persecutors and temptation. Lapide: the "evil man" (homo malus) = primarily the devil, whose instrument human persecutors become.

CUM DEFECIT EX ME SPIRITUS MEUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 141 — "When my spirit failed me, then Thou knewest my paths." The prayer of complete desolation: the spirit failing within, no human support (v.5: "no man that would know me... no man that careth for my soul"). Lapide: this is Christ's experience at Gethsemane and the Cross — abandoned by the disciples, rejected by His people, apparently forsaken by the Father. And yet: "thou knewest my paths" — God knows the way even when the soul cannot see it.

ET NON INTRES IN IUDICIUM CUM SERVO TUO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 142 — the seventh and last penitential psalm. "And enter not into judgment with Thy servant: for in Thy sight no man living shall be justified." Quoted by Paul in Rom. 3:20 and Gal. 2:16 in the discussion of justification: no man is justified by works before God's strict tribunal. Lapide carefully distinguishes: this verse does not abolish the doctrine of merit (which is real but always depends on divine grace as its first cause); it only insists that no creature can stand before God's strict justice as though claiming a right independent of grace and mercy.

EXALTABO TE DEUS MEUS REX. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 144 — "I will extol Thee, O God, my King: and I will bless Thy name forever; yea, forever and ever." Lapide: the last of the acrostic psalms (Hebrew alphabet). The great psalm of divine kingship and praise that begins the final doxological sequence (Psalms 144-150). "Exaltabo" — I will EXALT Thee: the creature's praise elevates (exalts) the divine name not in reality (for God cannot be increased) but in the creature's own perception and proclamation.

LAUDA ANIMA MEA DOMINUM. Lapide: Psalm 145 — "Praise the Lord, O my soul, in my life I will praise the Lord: I will sing to my God as long as I shall be." Lapide: "in vita mea" — THROUGHOUT MY LIFE, not merely in moments of consolation. The commitment to perpetual praise as the animating principle of the Christian life. Lapide: "donec fuero" (as long as I shall be) — even beyond death, in eternal life, the praise continues and is perfected.

QUI SANAT CONTRITOS CORDE. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 146 — "Who healeth the broken of heart, and bindeth up their bruises." The divine Physician of the soul. Lapide: "contriti corde" — the broken-hearted: those crushed by grief, sin, failure, loss. Christ identifies Himself as the healer of broken hearts (Is. 61:1, cited in Lk. 4:18: "He hath sent Me to heal the broken-hearted"). The Church's ministry of consolation — especially through the sacrament of Penance and the pastoral care of souls — is the continuation of this divine healing.

LAUDATE DOMINUM DE CAELIS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 148 — the great cosmic praise psalm. "Praise ye the Lord from the heavens: praise ye Him in the high places." The call to praise descends through all creation: angels (v.2), sun and moon (v.3), stars (v.3), heaven of heavens (v.4), waters above the heavens (v.4) — then earthly creation: sea monsters and deeps (v.7), fire and hail (v.8), mountains and hills (v.9), trees and animals (v.10) — finally men (vv.11-12). Lapide: the entire cosmos as one great choir of divine praise, with man as the conscious conductor who brings to explicit articulation what all creation performs by its very existence.

CANTATE DOMINO CANTICUM NOVUM. Lapide: Psalm 149 — "Sing ye to the Lord a new canticle." The "new canticle" repeated from Ps. 97: the song of the New Covenant. Lapide: "Laus eius in ecclesia sanctorum" (v.1) — His praise in the church of the holy ones: the proper place of praise is the Church, the assembly of the saints. The individual's praise is completed and perfected in the liturgical assembly.

LAUDATE DOMINUM IN SANCTIS EIUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 150 — the great doxological conclusion of the entire Psalter. "Praise ye the Lord in His holy places: praise Him in the firmament of His power." Lapide: the six-verse crescendo of praise is the liturgical summit of the Psalter and of all Scripture. "Laudate eum in virtutibus eius" (v.2) — praise Him for His mighty acts (the works of creation, Providence, Redemption). Lapide: the multiple instruments of vv.3-5 (trumpet, psaltery, harp, timbrel, strings, organs, cymbals) = the totality of human artistic expression offered to God in praise. The Church's music — Gregorian chant, polyphony, organ, all sacred music — is the fulfillment of this verse.

DOMINE DEUS MEUS IN TE SPERAVI. Lapide: Psalm 7 — a prayer for deliverance from enemies. David protests his innocence before God. Lapide: the appeal to God as judge (v.9: "judge me, O Lord, according to my justice") is not presumption but confidence in God's knowledge of interior truth — He alone sees what men cannot see. Lapide: allegorically Christ appealing to the Father in His Passion, protesting the injustice of His condemnation.

CONFITEBOR TIBI DOMINE IN TOTO CORDE MEO. Lapide: Psalm 9 — "I will give praise to Thee, O Lord, with my whole heart." The wholeness of praise demanded: the whole heart (totum cor), not divided, not partial. Lapide: half-hearted praise is no praise at all. The soul's faculties (memory, intellect, will) must all be engaged in the act of praise.

QUONIAM ECCE PECCATORES INTENDERUNT ARCUM. Lapide: Psalm 10 — "For lo, the wicked have bent their bow: they have prepared their arrows in the quiver." The image of the wicked as archers lying in ambush. Lapide: the "arrows" of the wicked = their words (detraction, slander), their actions (persecution), their counsels (temptation). Lapide: "the just man" (v.3: "what has he done") is helpless against such attacks by natural means — his only resource is to "flee to the mountain" (v.1: God's protection).

SALVUM ME FAC DOMINE QUONIAM DEFECIT SANCTUS. Lapide: Psalm 11 — "Save me, O Lord, for there is now no saint: truths are decayed from among the children of men." Lapide: the lament that saints are rare and truth has declined — applicable to every age of the Church but especially to times of cultural and moral collapse. Lapide: "Vana locuti sunt unusquisque ad proximum suum" (v.3: "They have spoken vain things every one to his neighbor") — the corruption of speech as a symptom of moral decline.

USQUEQUO DOMINE OBLIVISCERIS ME IN FINEM. Lapide: Psalm 12 — "How long, O Lord, wilt Thou forget me unto the end?" The prayer of desolation. Lapide: God does not literally forget, but the soul in affliction experiences divine silence as abandonment. The "how long" (usquequo) repeated four times in two verses = the anguish of prolonged waiting. Lapide: this psalm is the model of persevering prayer in the dark night of the soul, ending (v.6) with sudden confidence: "But I have trusted in Thy mercy."

DIXIT INSIPIENS IN CORDE SUO NON EST DEUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 13 — "The fool hath said in his heart: There is no God." Paul quotes vv.1-3 in Rom. 3:10-12 as demonstrating universal sinfulness. Lapide: the "fool" (insipiens) = not the intellectual atheist primarily but the practical atheist — the man who LIVES as though God does not exist, regardless of what he professes with his lips. Lapide: "in corde suo" — in his HEART, not with his mouth. Practical atheism is more widespread and more dangerous than theoretical atheism.

DOMINE QUIS HABITABIT IN TABERNACULO TUO. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 14 — "Lord, who shall dwell in Thy tabernacle? Or who shall rest in Thy holy hill?" The conditions for dwelling with God: listed in vv.2-5. Lapide follows Augustine's commentary: the conditions (integrity of life, justice, truth of heart, no deceit, no evil, honor to the God-fearing, fidelity to oaths, no usury, no bribes) are the description of the just man. Christ is the ONE who perfectly fulfills all conditions; every Christian in proportion to their charity participates.

EXAUDI DOMINE IUSTITIAM MEAM. Lapide: Psalm 16 — "Hear, O Lord, my justice: attend to my supplication." David protests his integrity before God. Lapide: the boldness of this appeal rests not on sinlessness but on the specific righteousness of the cause David pleads (innocence of this particular accusation). Lapide: the soul can appeal to God with boldness not from self-righteousness but from confidence in divine mercy and the righteousness imputed through grace.

DOMINUS ILLUMINATIO MEA ET SALUS MEA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 26 — "The Lord is my light and my salvation: whom shall I fear? The Lord is the protector of my life: of whom shall I be afraid?" One of the most beloved confessional verses in the Psalter. Lapide: the three divine attributes — light (illuminatio), salvation (salus), protector of life (protector vitae) — correspond to the three theological virtues: faith (light), hope (salvation), charity (life's protector). The two rhetorical questions ("whom shall I fear? of whom shall I be afraid?") express the absolute confidence of the soul that has God.

AD TE DOMINE CLAMABO. Lapide: Psalm 27 — "To Thee, O Lord, will I cry: O my God, be not Thou silent to me." Lapide: the prayer that God not be silent = the prayer that God respond with His word, His providence, His consolation. The divine silence is the most terrifying experience of prayer; Lapide: when God seems silent, He is most deeply present — purifying the soul through the dark night.

VOX DOMINI SUPER AQUAS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 28 — the great theophany psalm. "The voice of the Lord is upon the waters: the God of majesty hath thundered, the Lord is upon many waters." The "voice of the Lord" (Vulgate: seven times in vv.3-9) = the thunder. Lapide gives a natural theology of thunder: the overwhelming power of the storm is a visible parable of divine omnipotence. Lapide: "vox Domini" is also the divine Word — the eternal Son — spoken over the primordial waters (Gen. 1:2), spoken at Christ's baptism (Mt. 3:17), and spoken in Scripture and Tradition.

AD VESPERUM DEMORABITUR FLETUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 29 — "In the evening weeping shall have place, and in the morning gladness." The theology of spiritual rhythm: alternation of consolation and desolation is the normal pattern of the spiritual life. Lapide: "weeping endures for a night but joy comes in the morning" — the Passion (night/evening) and the Resurrection (morning). Tropologically: every dark night of the soul, every spiritual desolation, is followed by the dawn of renewed consolation. Lapide: the mystics (John of the Cross especially) elaborate this rhythm as the "dark night" leading to "divine union."

IN MANUS TUAS COMMENDO SPIRITUM MEUM. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 30 — "Into Thy hands I commend my spirit." Christ's last words on the Cross (Lk. 23:46). Lapide: David used these words as a prayer before sleep; Christ uses them as a prayer in death. The perfect act of entrustment: the soul handed over completely to God. Lapide: this is the model of a holy death — the final act of faith and love that commends the soul into God's hands. The Church uses this verse in the prayer for the dying (the Commendation of a Soul).

SI VERE UTIQUE IUSTITIAM LOQUIMINI. Lapide: Psalm 57 — "If in very deed you speak justice, judge uprightly, O ye sons of men." The indictment of corrupt judges and rulers. Lapide: the "sons of men" in positions of authority are called to justice. "In corde iniquitatem operamini" (v.3: "In your hearts you work iniquity") — corruption begins in the heart, not in the outward act. Lapide gives this psalm a political reading: the perversion of justice by those in authority is a grave sin that cries to heaven.

ERIPE ME DE INIMICIS MEIS DEUS MEUS. Lapide: Psalm 58 — "Deliver me from my enemies, O my God." Lapide: literally David's prayer when Saul sent men to watch his house and kill him (1 Sam. 19:11). Allegorically: Christ in the Passion surrounded by enemies. Tropologically: every Christian surrounded by temptation, persecution, and sin.

DA NOBIS AUXILIUM DE TRIBULATIONE. Lapide: Psalm 59 — "Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man." Lapide: "vana salus hominis" — the vanity of all human help when God withdraws His support. The lesson of military defeat (the psalm is titled after a defeat of Israel): God permits defeat to teach the people that the victory is His, not theirs. Lapide: this is the theology of the Cross — apparent defeat that is actually victory through divine power.

A FINIBUS TERRAE AD TE CLAMAVI. Lapide: Psalm 60 — "From the ends of the earth I have cried to Thee, when my heart was in anguish." Lapide: the psalm of the Church in exile — "from the ends of the earth" = the furthest reaches of the world where the Church has been scattered. Allegorically: Christ's prayer in His distance from the Father (the dereliction). The "rock" to which David wants to be elevated (v.3: "set me up on a rock") = Christ, the Rock of ages.

NONNE DEO SUBIECTA ERIT ANIMA MEA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 61 — "Shall not my soul be subject to God? For from Him is my salvation." Lapide: the soul's subjection (subiecta) to God is not servitude but the proper order of being — the creature rightly ordered to the Creator. The submission is simultaneously the source of salvation: from this subjection comes (ex ipso) salvation. Lapide: "ipse Deus meus et salutare meum" (v.2: He is my God and my Saviour) — the double identification: God as the soul's proper object AND as its Saviour.

EXAUDI DEUS ORATIONEM MEAM CUM DEPRECOR. Lapide: Psalm 63 — "Hear, O God, my prayer when I make supplication to Thee." Lapide: the simple beginning of prayer — asking God to hear. Lapide: the confidence of prayer is itself a grace: without God's gift of the spirit of prayer, the soul would not even begin to pray.

TE DECET HYMNUS DEUS IN SION. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 64 — "A hymn, O God, becometh Thee in Sion." Lapide: "te decet" — it is FITTING for Thee: praise is not arbitrary but ontologically appropriate to God's nature. "And a vow shall be paid to Thee in Jerusalem." Lapide: the conjunction of hymn (praise) and vow (promise): the soul that praises God commits itself to the fulfillment of its baptismal promises. Praise and obligation are inseparable.

IUBILATE DEO OMNIS TERRA PSALMUM DICITE NOMINI EIUS. Lapide: Psalm 65 — "Sing joyfully to God, all the earth, sing ye a psalm to His name." The universal call to liturgical praise. Lapide: "psalmum dicite" — sing a PSALM (not merely a hymn of one's own composition): the inspired prayers of the Psalter are the model and norm of all Christian praise. The Church's use of the Psalms in the Divine Office ensures that human prayer is always normed by divine inspiration.

DEUS MISEREATUR NOSTRI ET BENEDICAT NOBIS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 66 — "May God have mercy on us and bless us: may He cause the light of His countenance to shine upon us." The Aaronic blessing (Nu. 6:24-26) in psalm form. Lapide: "et misereatur nostri" — mercy and blessing together: mercy for sin, blessing for the just. The "light of his countenance" = the beatific vision, now given by partial anticipation in prayer, given fully in heaven. Lapide: this psalm is a model for intercession — praying for the whole people.

DEUS AUTEM REX NOSTER ANTE SAECULA. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 73 — "But God is our King before ages: He hath wrought salvation in the midst of the earth." Lapide: "ante saecula" — before the ages, before all time: the divine kingship is eternal, not merely historical. "Operatus est salutem in medio terrae" — He has worked salvation IN THE MIDST OF THE EARTH: the Incarnation as the central event of all history, the axis around which all time revolves. Lapide: "in medio" = at the center, geographically (Jerusalem) and temporally (the fullness of time, Gal. 4:4) and cosmically.

ET FACTUS EST IN PACE LOCUS EIUS. Lapide: Psalm 75 — "And His place is in peace: and His abode in Sion." Lapide: God dwells in peace — His essence is peace (the perfect self-sufficiency and harmony of the Trinity). Sion as God's dwelling = the Church, the new Sion. Lapide: the Church's primary vocation is to be a place of divine peace — an anticipation of the eternal peace of heaven.

HAEC MUTATIO DEXTERAE EXCELSI. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 76 — "This is the change of the right hand of the Most High." Lapide: the soul in desolation (vv.2-10: "Will God cast off forever?") arrives at the turning point: "this is the change of the right hand of the Most High" — the apparent abandonment is not rejection but a change (mutatio) worked by God's own hand. Lapide: the dark night, the apparent withdrawal of consolation, is itself a form of divine activity — God is changing the soul, not abandoning it. The right hand (dextera Excelsi) = God's power working precisely through the experience of weakness.

QUI REGIS ISRAEL INTENDE. Lapide: Psalm 79 — "Give ear, O Thou that rulest Israel: Thou that leadest Joseph like a sheep." The image of God as Shepherd of Israel, leading his people like sheep. Lapide: "qui sedes super Cherubim" (who sittest above the Cherubim) — the divine transcendence (above the highest angelic order) combined with the pastoral intimacy of leading sheep. The combination of transcendence and condescension is the mystery of God's dealing with His people.

CONFITEMINI DOMINO ET INVOCATE NOMEN EIUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 104 — the great historical psalm retelling the story of salvation from Abraham to the Exodus. "Give glory to the Lord, and call upon His name: declare His deeds among the Gentiles." Lapide: "annuntiate inter gentes opera eius" — the missionary dimension built into the very telling of salvation history. Israel's memory of God's deeds is not a private treasure but a gift to be proclaimed. Lapide: the Church's tradition (the anamnesis in the liturgy) is this continuous proclamation of the mirabilia Dei.

CONFITEMINI DOMINO QUONIAM BONUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 105 — "Praise ye the Lord, for He is good: for His mercy endureth forever." The great refrain of the Psalter (cf. Ps. 135; 1 Chron. 16:34; 2 Chron. 7:3; Ezra 3:11). Lapide: "quoniam bonus" — the reason for praise is not what God has done but what He IS: His goodness (bonitas) is the ground of all gratitude and praise. "Et misericordia eius in saeculum" — His mercy endures unto the age: permanence that outlasts every historical change.

CONFITEMINI DOMINO QUONIAM BONUS. Lapide: Psalm 106 — another great meditation on God's mercy in history. Four groups of rescued people: desert wanderers (vv.4-9), prisoners (vv.10-16), the sick (vv.17-22), storm-tossed sailors (vv.23-32). Lapide: each group represents a type of spiritual condition — disorientation, bondage, sickness, storm — from which God rescues those who cry to Him. Lapide: "clamabunt ad Dominum... et de necessitatibus eorum eripuit eos" (v.6: "they cried to the Lord... and He delivered them from their distresses") — the cry, the hearing, the deliverance: the pattern of all prayer answered.

EXSURGE GLORIA MEA EXSURGE PSALTERIUM ET CITHARA. Lapide: Psalm 107 — "Arise, O my glory: arise, psaltery and harp." The soul's instruments of praise — the psaltery (spiritual understanding) and harp (bodily voice and action). Lapide: the two together = the integration of interior and exterior praise. The soul that sings only with the lips without understanding is half-mute; the soul that understands without giving voice leaves creation's praise incomplete.

PRO DILIGENTIBUS ME DETRAHEBANT MIHI. Lapide: Psalm 108 — the great imprecatory psalm (NT quoted in Acts 1:20). "Instead of making me a return of love, they detracted me." Lapide: quoted of Judas in Acts 1:20 ("his bishopric let another take," v.8). The betrayal of love by hatred is the most acute form of injustice — and the one Christ most perfectly exemplifies in His suffering.

PRETIOSA IN CONSPECTU DOMINI MORS SANCTORUM EIUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 115 — "Precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of His saints." One of the most consoling verses in the Psalter. Lapide: "pretiosa" — precious, costly (pretium = price): the death of the saints is of infinite value in God's sight because it is united to the death of Christ. Lapide: the martyr's death is precious because it participates in the supreme sacrifice of the Cross. The death of every faithful Christian — who dies in a state of grace — is precious because it brings the soul to the God it has loved. Lapide: this verse is central to the Catholic theology of the "happy death" — the death sought, prepared for, and offered to God as the soul's final and definitive act of love.

LAPIDEM QUEM REPROBAVERUNT AEDIFICANTES. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 117 — "The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner." The most frequently cited verse in the NT from the Psalter: Mt. 21:42; Mk. 12:10-11; Lk. 20:17; Acts 4:11; 1 Pet. 2:7. Lapide: the rejected stone = Christ rejected by the Jewish leaders (the builders of the Temple and the theocratic state); become the cornerstone = the foundation of the Church and of the entire order of salvation. The paradox: what human builders discard, God makes the most important structural element. Lapide: "a Domino factum est istud et est mirabile in oculis nostris" — this is the Lord's doing and it is wonderful in our eyes: the Resurrection as the miracle that transforms rejection into foundation.

LAUDATE NOMEN DOMINI LAUDATE SERVI DOMINUM. Lapide: Psalm 134 — "Praise ye the name of the Lord: O you His servants, praise the Lord." The liturgical assembly called to praise. Lapide: "qui statis in domo Domini in atriis domus Dei nostri" (who stand in the house of the Lord, in the courts of the house of our God) = the clergy and religious whose specific vocation is the Divine Office. Their praise is the center of the Church's life and the model for all Christian prayer.

CONFITEMINI DOMINO QUONIAM BONUS. # GEM Lapide: Psalm 135 — the great litany of divine mercy. Each verse ends with "quoniam in aeternum misericordia eius" (for His mercy endureth forever). Twenty-six verses: 26 times the divine mercy is proclaimed eternal. Lapide: the repetition is not literary tedium but theological insistence — the eternal mercy of God is the ground of all history, all creation, all redemption. The structure (creation in vv.5-9, Exodus in vv.10-16, conquest in vv.17-22, and present care in vv.23-25) shows that divine mercy spans every event of salvation history.
